coredata.dc:title: A mechanism for excitation-dependent emission from carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Mondal, Manisha
Pramanik, Subhamay
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CDs) synthesized via the microwave pyrolysis approach are monodisperse with the nano-size distribution. The CDs show color tunability simply by changing the excitation wavelength. Also, the emission intensity varies with varying different microwave response times. Detail characterizations indicate that the carbon defects that lead to heterogeneous emission levels are responsible for the emission properties of the CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Microwave Pyrolysis method
Emission
Surface defects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (i). TEM image (ii) AFM topography image of 8-CDs, (iii) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs.
(i) FTIR spectra of CDs, (ii) PL emission spectra of 4-CDs.

(i) PL emission spectra of CDs upon 430 nm excitation wavelength {Inset shows the photograph of 4-, 6-, and 8-CDs (left to right) under normal (upper) light and 305 nm (bottom) light exposure} (ii) Raman spectra of 4-,6-, and 8-CDs.

head.ce:title: A mechanism for excitation-dependent emission from carbon nanodots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) synthesized via the microwave pyrolysis approach are monodisperse with the nano-size distribution. The CDs show color tunability simply by changing the excitation wavelength. Also, the emission intensity varies with varying different microwave response times. Detail characterizations indicate that the carbon defects that lead to heterogeneous emission levels are responsible for the emission properties of the CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A wide range of luminescent nanomaterials has found increasing concern in optoelectronics, light emitting devices, nanosensors, bio-imaging, etc., in the past few years [1–9]. Carbon nanodots (CDs) are the most commonly used luminescent nanomaterials because of their outstanding photoluminescence (PL) characteristics, less toxicity, water solubility, and good biocompatibility [7,10]. So far, various synthesis routes have been established, for instance, the chemical oxidation of soot, the hydrothermal method, the electrochemical oxidation of graphite, and the microwave-assisted method for preparing the luminescent CDs. The PL mechanism of the CDs is rather complex. To explain this phenomenon several possible reasons reported, such as the quantum-confinement effect, different emissive traps on the surface, defect states, doping, surface passivation, and solvent polarity etc [10–14]. Many studies based on the excitation-dependent PL properties of carbon dots have been reported [6,15–17]. Sun et al. reported surface defect-based PL emission of CDs upon surface passivation by PEG1500N [17]. They have demonstrated the quantum confinement effect of emissive energy traps on the surface of CDs. The carbon nanodot's excitation-dependent PL emission property is due to the surface defect states near the Fermi level.

In the present work, Cardon nanodots (CDs) were synthesized using the microwave pyrolysis method by varying microwave reaction times [18]. We characterized the CDs by conducting X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, UV–VIS spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and PL measurements. The PL emission mechanism of the CDs has been explained based on FTIR and Raman spectra.
For the synthesis of CDs through the microwave pyrolysis method, purified dextrose anhydrous and ethylene glycol 400 (Merck, purity 99%, synthesis grade) are used.

In this method, at first, a solution of ethylene glycol, glucose, and DI water was mixed, then stirred for 30 min at room temperature to make a uniform transparent solution. After that, the mixed solution was heated in a microwave oven at a power of 540 W (LG, MC2844EB) for three different durations viz., 4 min, 6 min, and 8 min respectively. The solution changed its color from colorless to yellow. Finally, the products obtained from the 4-, 6-, and 8-minute microwave treatments are designated as 4-CDs, 6-CDs, and 8-CDs respectively.

The details of characterization techniques are provided in electronic supplementary materials (ESM). From the XRD study (ESM Fig. 1 ), a broad peak centered at 23.20˚ indicates the nano size as well as the disordered structures of the CDs. This broad peak at 23.20˚ corresponds to the (002) plane [19]. The formation of nanosized dots has been validated by TEM characterization techniques. Fig. 1(i) depicts a representative TEM image of 8-CDs with an average size of 5–6 nm.

Furthermore, the topographic information of 8-CDs is demonstrated by AFM observation which also confirms the particles are spherical in shape, monodisperse, and have narrow height distribution {Fig. 1(ii)}. The optical absorption spectra of 4-CDs, 6-CDs, and 8-CDs are shown in Fig. 1(iii). The intense peak centered at 273 nm is ascribed to π → π* transition of C=C, besides the weak intense peak at 339 nm occurs due to n → π* transition of the C=O bond [11]. To know the surface state of the CDs, FTIR spectra were attained {Fig. 2 (i)}. The dips in the range of 1000–1300 cm−1 are assigned to C-OH stretching and OH bending vibrations [7]. The dips at 1357 cm−1 and 2870 cm−1 are corresponding to C-H stretching vibration while the dip that appears at 3441 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of the C–OH group [6,7]. The dip lies at 1650 cm−1 attributed to the C=C vibration [7]. Thus, the FTIR spectra confirm the presence of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of all CDs which endorses the biocompatibility of the CDs.

The emission spectra have been recorded with varying the excitation wavelength from 330 nm to 470 nm, shown in Fig. 2(ii) for the 4-CDs sample. The spectra show the highest intense peak at 491 nm at the excitation wavelength of 430 nm. The color of 4-CDs and 6-CDs aqueous solutions are transparent while the 8-CDs aqueous solution is dark yellow under normal light exposure whereas they exhibit bright blue and bluish-green luminescence under the excitation of 405 nm {inset of Fig. 3 (i)}. When the microwave reaction time has increased the characteristics of the emission bands are the same but the intensities of all the bands decrease {Fig. 3(i)}. This may be due to the surface defects of CDs which are confirmed by the FTIR results. Compared to the 8-CDs, 4-CDs exhibit a pointedly sturdy absorption band at 3441 cm−1 of the C–OH band. To comprehend the structural changes, the Raman spectra of the three CDs are recorded {Fig. 3(ii)}, which show two prominent peaks around 1290 cm−1 and 1592 cm−1, commonly known as the D band and G bands of CDs respectively. The disorder D band is associated with defects in the sp3 carbon while the G band is accompanying sp2 hybridized carbon networks [20]. The intensity ratio of the D band to the G band in the Raman spectra indicates the high degree of carbonization of the CDs [20]. The intensity ratio of the D band to G band of the 4-CDs is found to be 0.63 and it gradually increases with the microwave reaction time, signifying that the defects increase possibly due to the oxidation and hetero groups on the surface [20].

Furthermore, the emission spectra display typical excitation-dependent characteristics, shown in Fig. 2(ii). The excitation wavelength gradually upsurges from 330 nm to 470 nm, and the emission peak shifts towards a longer wavelength. Because of the surface oxidation, surface defect states occur which generates miscellaneous emission sites onto CDs. Hence color-tunable emission generates in the CDs. The other two samples (6- and 8-CDs) exhibit almost the same PL spectra (not shown here) as those obtained from 4-CDs.

In summary, the emission from CDs synthesized via the Pyrolysis approach at different microwave reaction times is investigated. With increasing the reaction time, the characteristics of all the spectra remain unchanged but the blue emission deteriorates which is supported by the increment in the intensity of the C-OH band in the FTIR spectra. Based on the Raman spectroscopy and PL behavior carbon defects are responsible for the excitation-dependent emission characteristics.

Manisha Mondal: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Subhamay Pramanik: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Resources, Visualization, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors highly acknowledge Department of physics, Sidho-Kanho-Birsha University for providing PL, Raman, and UV facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mlblux.2023.100195.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biocompatible carbon dots with diverse surface modification
